questions.prompt: Which of these is generally considered a "need" rather than a "want"?
options: The newest phone model
Gas money to get to your job
Concert tickets
questions.explanation: Getting to work reliably is a need — the other two are wants, even if they feel important.

questions.prompt: In the 50/30/20 budgeting rule, what does the 20% usually go toward?
options: Wants
Needs
Savings or paying off debt
questions.explanation: The 20% slice is for savings and debt payoff — the part of your budget that builds your future.

questions.prompt: What does it mean when prices "inflate" over time?
options: Prices generally rise, so money buys less over time
Prices generally fall
Nothing changes about prices
questions.explanation: Inflation means rising prices over time, which means the same amount of money buys less in the future than it does today.

questions.prompt: What's the main purpose of an emergency fund?
options: Buying things on sale
Covering unexpected, necessary costs
Investing in the stock market
questions.explanation: Emergency funds exist for unplanned necessary expenses — not sales, wants, or investing.

questions.prompt: What's the difference between gross pay and net pay?
options: There's no real difference
Gross pay is before taxes/withholdings; net pay is what actually lands in your account
Net pay is always higher than gross pay
questions.explanation: Gross pay is your full earnings before anything is taken out; net pay is what's left after taxes and other withholdings.

questions.prompt: What typically happens if you spend more money than what's in your checking account with overdraft coverage on?
options: Nothing happens
The purchase may go through, but you're usually charged a fee
Your bank account is automatically closed
questions.explanation: Overdraft coverage can let a purchase go through even without enough funds, but typically comes with a real fee.

questions.prompt: What's the main difference between a debit card and a credit card?
options: A debit card spends money you already have; a credit card is borrowed money you repay later
They work exactly the same way
Credit cards can only be used in stores
questions.explanation: Debit pulls from your own balance right now. Credit is a loan you're expected to pay back.

questions.prompt: You put $100 in a savings account that earns interest. What does "earning interest" mean?
options: The bank pays you extra money over time just for keeping your money there
You have to pay the bank extra money
The $100 disappears and is replaced with stock
questions.explanation: Interest is money the bank pays you for keeping your funds with them — your balance grows over time just by sitting there.

questions.prompt: Which of these best describes "opportunity cost"?
options: The listed price of an item
Whatever you give up by choosing to spend money on one thing instead of another
A discount you get for buying in bulk
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is what you give up by choosing one option — every dollar spent one way can't be spent another way.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a realistic effective hourly rate calculation for a side hustle that earns $30 profit over 3 total hours (including setup and cleanup)?
options: $30/hour
$10/hour
$3/hour
questions.explanation: $30 ÷ 3 hours = $10/hour — dividing total profit by total time (not just the fun parts) gives the real hourly value.
